title: Air India's Fleet Reset: A350s, 787s and a Modernisation Push
dek: Under Tata ownership, Air India is overhauling cabins and growing a widebody fleet anchored by the A350 and 787. Where the transformation stands now.
body:
Air India's multi-year transformation under the Tata Group remains one of the most consequential fleet and product stories in Asian aviation. The carrier has placed one of the largest aircraft orders in commercial aviation history, spanning Airbus and Boeing widebodies and narrowbodies, while simultaneously refurbishing older jets and integrating Vistara following their merger.

A widebody backbone

At the centre of the long-haul strategy are the Airbus A350 and the Boeing 787 Dreamliner, complemented by an existing 777 fleet on the densest routes. The A350, with its composite airframe and modern Rolls-Royce Trent XWB engines, gives Air India a quiet, fuel-efficient platform for ultra-long sectors, while the 787 offers flexibility on a broad mix of medium and long-haul markets.

New-delivery aircraft arrive with updated cabins, but a significant share of the legacy widebody fleet still requires retrofitting. The pace of that retrofit programme, which involves seats, in-flight entertainment and interiors, is a key determinant of how quickly passengers experience a consistent product.

Narrowbody growth and integration

On shorter routes, the A320neo family underpins domestic and regional expansion, supporting Air India's ambition to strengthen its position in one of the world's fastest-growing aviation markets. The integration of Vistara has added aircraft, crews and a premium service heritage that Air India aims to carry into its mainline brand.

Several themes will define the coming period:

  • Speed of widebody cabin retrofits across the legacy fleet
  • Delivery timing for new A350s and 787s amid industry-wide supply constraints
  • Network growth on long-haul routes to Europe and North America

The transformation is ambitious and inherently gradual, with supply chain pressures affecting deliveries across the industry. Yet the direction is clear: a modern, widebody-led fleet intended to reposition Air India among the leading full-service carriers in Asia.
